name: Moses Coulee
short_description: Moses Coulee offers rockhounds access to agate and chalcedony specimens in the semi-arid Columbia Plateau region. This moderate-difficulty site features exposed geological formations typical of Cen...
description:
Moses Coulee is a scenic drainage in Douglas County, Washington, located within the Columbia Plateau physiographic province. The area features moderate slopes with sagebrush and sparse vegetation, typical of the rain shadow east of the Cascade Mountains. Rockhounders visit this location to search for agate nodules, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens that weather out of the surrounding basaltic and sedimentary layers.

The geological history of this region reflects millions of years of volcanic activity. The Miocene-age Columbia River Basalt Group dominates the landscape, with interbedded sedimentary rocks that contain silica-rich deposits. These silica-rich fluids, concentrated during the long volcanic period, formed agate nodules and chalcedony replacements that collectors seek today. The moderate slopes and exposed cuts along washes make specimen hunting productive in this area.

Accessibility is straightforward from the Douglas County area, with parking near the coulee entrance. Expect moderate walking over uneven terrain with scattered rocks and sagebrush. The elevation of approximately 1,285 feet and semi-arid climate mean exposure to sun and wind, so plan accordingly. Best collecting occurs in exposed wash bottoms and hillside cuts where weathering naturally exposes new specimens.
history: Moses Coulee sits within the Columbia Plateau, shaped by Miocene-age volcanism (approximately 17-14 million years ago) when the Columbia River Basalt Group erupted across eastern Washington. The interbedded silica-rich sedimentary layers within and between basalt flows created the agate nodules and chalcedony deposits that weather naturally along the coulee, making this area a productive informal rockhounding destination.
tips: Search the exposed wash bottoms and eroded hillside banks where basalt and sedimentary rocks break down naturally - this is where new specimens weather out. Bring a hand lens to examine partially exposed specimens for color quality before extracting. Look for white, gray, and banded agate nodules ranging from walnut-sized to fist-sized; chalcedony often appears as waxy, translucent fragments on the wash surface. Early morning collecting before afternoon winds is most productive, and always scan the ground systematically rather than digging randomly.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1,285 feet in the semi-arid Columbia Plateau. Moderate slopes with scattered basalt rocks, sagebrush, and sparse grass cover. Uneven footing typical of a natural coulee with loose rock, sandy wash bottoms, and eroded banks. Walking distance from parking area to productive zones typically 0.5 to 2 miles depending on access point. No established trail; cross-country hiking over native terrain required.
best_season: April - June, September - October. Spring and fall offer moderate temperatures ideal for hiking and collecting. Summer heat (often above 85 degrees Fahrenheit) combined with full sun exposure makes mid-day collecting uncomfortable. Winter brings occasional snow and frozen ground in higher areas.
written_directions: From the town of Coulee City (approximately 20 miles south), take WA-174 north toward the Moses Coulee area. Watch for pullouts and access points along or near the coulee rim. Alternatively, from the Douglas County area near Reardan or Airway Heights, head east on local roads toward the coulee drainage. Specific access points may require local knowledge; contact the BLM Spokane Field Office for current recommended parking and entry locations.
road_conditions: Paved highway access to area; final approach roads may be unpaved gravel or dirt. Check conditions seasonally as spring runoff can impact access roads.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along coulee rim or near access points. Limited developed parking; typically 2-4 vehicle spaces at established spots. No amenities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy machinery. Collecting limited to loose specimens and weathered fragments; do not break in-place formations or remove entire outcrops. Leave no trace and pack out all non-collectible waste.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA

No motorized excavation

Do not break in-place formations
Leave no trace
nearest_services: Coulee City (approximately 20 miles south) offers gas, food, and water services. Smaller services may be available in nearby communities.
accessibility_notes: Moderate difficulty terrain with uneven footing, scattered rocks, and loose material. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Significant sun exposure with minimal shade; bring water and sun protection. Moderate physical fitness required for walking distances and navigating rocky slopes.

faq.question: What rocks can I find rockhounding at Moses Coulee, Washington?
faq.answer: Moses Coulee is known for agate nodules, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens. The semi-arid terrain and exposed basalt slopes make it an excellent location for finding banded agate and white to gray chalcedony fragments, particularly in wash bottoms where natural weathering exposes new material.

faq.question: Is Moses Coulee open to public rockhounding without a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, Moses Coulee is on public BLM land and is open to free, permit-free rockhounding. Collectors must follow personal use guidelines: maximum 25 lbs per day, no commercial collection, and no motorized excavation. This makes it an accessible site for both beginners and experienced rockhounders.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Moses Coulee?
faq.answer: April through June and September through October offer the most comfortable collecting conditions. Spring and fall temperatures avoid summer heat (often above 85 degrees Fahrenheit) and provide reliable ground conditions. Winter can bring occasional snow, making access unpredictable.

faq.question: How difficult is it to collect at Moses Coulee, and is it family-friendly?
faq.answer: Moses Coulee is rated moderate difficulty, making it suitable for families with children ages 8 and up. The terrain involves uneven footing and loose rock over a semi-arid landscape, but does not require technical climbing. Kids can successfully spot and collect specimens with adult supervision.

faq.question: What tools do I need to go rockhounding at Moses Coulee?
faq.answer: Bring a hand lens to examine specimens before extracting them, a small pick or chisel for loosening specimens, and a collection bag for specimens up to 25 lbs. Sturdy hiking boots, sun protection, and at least 2 liters of water are essential due to full sun exposure and lack of shade on the semi-arid terrain.

faq.question: Where exactly should I look for agate at Moses Coulee?
faq.answer: Focus your search on wash bottoms and naturally eroded hillside banks where basalt and sedimentary rocks break down and expose new material. Early morning collecting yields better results. Look for white, gray, and banded specimens ranging from walnut to fist-sized, and examine the ground systematically rather than random digging.

meta_title: Moses Coulee Rockhounding - Find Agate in Washington
meta_description: Free rockhounding at Moses Coulee, Washington for agate, chalcedony, and jasper. Public BLM land, no permit required. Best April-June and September-October.
